questionText: A factory produces steel rods whose lengths, $X$ cm, are modelled by $X \sim N(20, 0.5^2)$. A rod is scrapped if its length is less than $19$ cm, in which case the whole rod is wasted. Each rod costs £2 to produce. In a production run of $8000$ rods, estimate the number of rods scrapped and hence the expected total cost of the scrapped rods.

steps.description: State the distribution, batch size and cost
steps.explanation: Rod lengths are normal with mean $20$ cm and standard deviation $0.5$ cm. We will find the probability a rod is scrapped, scale up to $8000$ rods, then attach the cost. Each rod costs $2$ pounds to make.
A rod is scrapped precisely when its length falls below $19$ cm, so the scrap rate is the probability that $X$ is less than $19$.
We convert the cut-off length $19$ into a $z$-score by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation.
The length $19$ cm lies exactly two standard deviations below the mean.
The area to the left of $z = -2$ equals the area to the right of $z = +2$, which is $1 - \Phi(2)$, by the symmetry of the bell curve.
The cumulative normal at $z = 2$ gives the fraction of rods shorter than two standard deviations above the mean.
About $2.3\%$ of rods are too short and must be scrapped.

steps.description: Set up the expected number scrapped
steps.explanation: For a large independent batch the expected number of scrapped rods is the batch size times the scrap probability.
Multiplying gives the expected count of scrapped rods in the run.

steps.description: Round to a whole number of rods
steps.explanation: Rods are whole items, so we round to the nearest whole rod.

steps.description: Link the count to a cost
steps.explanation: Every scrapped rod is a total loss of its $2$-pound production cost, so the expected wasted cost is the number of scrapped rods multiplied by $2$.
Carrying out the multiplication gives the expected money wasted on scrapped rods, in pounds.

steps.description: State the expected total scrap cost
steps.explanation: This is the amount the factory can expect to lose to scrapped rods in a run of $8000$.
Combining the scrap rate, batch size and unit cost in one line reproduces the same figure, confirming the two-stage calculation is consistent.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: About $182$ rods are scrapped ($8000 \times 0.02275$), giving an expected scrap cost of about £364.
